94CY-NVV: Born in Perthshire, Scotland, Cornelius Cargill crossed the Atlantic and built a life on the Virginia frontier. He patented hundreds of acres along the Roanoke River and beyond, served as executor and witness on neighbors' wills, and in 1746 was commissioned to help organize the new county of Lunenburg. He and his wife Elizabeth raised nine children before his death at the age of 83.
9QMM-G7T: John Kirby left Rowington in Warwickshire as a boy of twelve, sailing for New England aboard the Hopewell in 1635. He lived at Plymouth and Hartford before settling at Wethersfield and finally Middletown, Connecticut, where his homestead stood in a wooded bend of the river still known as Kirby's Bridge. He died in 1677 leaving a substantial estate and, by one account, no fewer than nine Bibles.
G54L-5LD: Mary Ann Sorrell's life traced a path across the colonies, with ties recorded in Virginia, Pennsylvania, and South Carolina, where she was living in 1768. The surviving records of her life are sparse, but they place her among the families who moved and resettled through the early American backcountry before her death in Pennsylvania in 1769.
GLNB-4XJ: Josepha Anna Bader was born and baptized in the village of Berg, in the Württemberg region of Germany, in 1683. Like many of her countrymen, she eventually crossed the ocean to Pennsylvania, where she lived out her later years in Delaware Township near Philadelphia until her death in 1750.
KHJF-LD9: A farmer from Essex, England, Francis Andrews came to New England in the 1630s and was settled in Hartford by 1639 as an early member of its First Church. His name endures on the Founder's Monument in Hartford's Ancient Burying Ground. Around 1648 he moved to Fairfield, Connecticut, where he and his wife Anna raised eight children before his death in 1663.
KNY6-KK8: Thomas Franklin Jones answered the call of the Revolution, serving as a captain of a militia company recruited along the Tiger River in South Carolina's 96th District. He was killed in a skirmish with British and Tory forces at Clark's Ford on the Enoree River on January 12, 1781, and was buried on the riverbank near where he fell.
L18T-VJV: Born at Jamestown in 1671 to a long-established colonial family, John Henry Chiles became a man of standing in early Virginia. He served as a vestryman of Saint Peter's Parish in New Kent County, a position of both church and civic authority. He raised a large family of sixteen children with his first wife before his death in Hanover County at the age of 47.
L2VR-5GZ: William Bramlett's name first appears in Essex County, Virginia, records in 1715, and over the following decades he moved westward through Middlesex, Caroline, and Lunenburg before settling in Bedford County. He is remembered there for Bramlett Road, the first road of importance in the county's records, an east-to-west thoroughfare he is believed to have helped build.
L4XX-ZKJ: A man of faith, Littleberry Strange was known to his community as a Reverend. Born in Lunenburg County, Virginia, in 1758, he lived through the era of the Revolution and eventually joined the migration south and west, ending his days in Amite County, Mississippi, in 1831.
L6F7-GFF: Benjamin Weeks Sr. began life in the Plymouth Colony at Falmouth and made his way south to the North Carolina coast, where he settled in White Oak Township in Carteret County. He set his affairs in order with a will written in November 1744, witnessed by his neighbors, which was proved the following summer after his death.
L8SD-32X: James Arnold spent his life along Mountain Creek in the Virginia backcountry, on land his family held as the colony's counties were repeatedly redrawn around them. He served a term of military duty in 1755 during the years of frontier conflict. He lived to see his home county of Mecklenburg carved out of Lunenburg, and died there in 1776 as the Revolution began.
LBJR-TQV: James Lindley Jr. left County Wicklow, Ireland, in 1713, arriving with his family and a Quaker certificate from the Carlow Meeting. He settled in Chester County, Pennsylvania, where he worked as a blacksmith and served as constable. He died young in 1726, leaving his wife Eleanor pregnant and a will naming a large family of children, most of them still minors.
LCF9-X6Q: Peter Webster came from Ingleton in Yorkshire, England, and made the long voyage to America in 1695, settling at Middletown in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. He spent the remainder of his life in that corner of the colony, dying at Bristol in 1724.
LD9G-684: One of the most remarkable figures of early America, Augustine Herman was born in Bohemia and came to New Amsterdam in the 1630s, where he prospered as a merchant. A gifted cartographer who spoke six languages, he served as diplomat, militia colonel, and justice. He created a famed map of the Chesapeake and was rewarded with vast lands at Bohemia Manor in Maryland, where he lived in baronial style until his death in 1686.
LHDM-56D: Captain Richard Barnes was born in Westmoreland County, Virginia, around 1680 and later made his home in Chowan County, North Carolina. His title reflects a life of standing in the colonial community. After his death in 1759, his estate was ordered divided among his children and the families they had married into.
LJ1G-835: Major Edward Dale arrived in Virginia in 1642, a cavalier in the turbulent years of the English Civil War. He established himself in Lancaster County, where he built Barford Plantation and, in 1674, gifted a 500-acre estate to his daughter Katherine and her husband Thomas Carter. He lived to about 75, leaving a will that was proved in 1695.
LKQ6-8J1: Thomas Brooke arrived in Maryland in 1650 with his father, Governor Robert Brooke, and rose to become one of the colony's leading men. He commanded the Calvert County militia as captain and major, served as High Sheriff, Burgess, and presiding Chief Justice, and patented thousands of acres of land. At his death in 1676 he held nearly 7,742 acres, and though raised Protestant, he had converted to Roman Catholicism.
LRRR-C6G: Robert Lamont Young was born in County Antrim in 1711 and married Agnes Crockett there before joining the great Scots-Irish migration to America. The family arrived in 1740 and settled in the Back Creek area of Augusta County, Virginia, part of the wave of Ulster families who pioneered the Shenandoah Valley. He died near Staunton around 1755.
LT8G-CZP: Baptized in Leicestershire, England, John Garrett II crossed to Virginia in the colony's early years and patented 300 acres in New Kent County in 1655. A restless settler, he later turned up in Maryland and then in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, his life tracing the spread of English families through the middle colonies before his death around 1728.
LVPS-64D: Thomas McGeehee's family carried a story of Scottish defiance: the name had once been MacGregor, an outlawed clan, and was changed to escape persecution. He came from Scotland to Virginia in the early 1650s and built up substantial holdings, owning land in New Kent by 1689 and later patenting acreage in King William County. He left a will in 1727 naming his many children.
LXSJ-7X6: James Richard Fletcher was a Virginia-born frontiersman who served as a captain in the War of 1812 and rose to colonel of the Georgia 42nd Militia Regiment. He helped survey the boundaries of Henry County, Georgia, when it was formed, and in 1822 became its first sheriff. He raised seven children with his wife Elizabeth before his sudden death.
LYDM-LL1: Laurent Gignard left La Rochelle, France, around 1657 and made his life in the young colony of New France. A master shoemaker by trade, he settled along the Côte-de-Beaupré near Quebec, where census records counted his family through the 1660s and 1670s. He died at Portneuf in 1702.
LZ6D-KYR: Jane Hunter was born at Rathdrum in County Wicklow, Ireland, and in 1717 joined the migration across the Atlantic, arriving in Pennsylvania. She settled in the colony she would call home for the remainder of her life, dying there in 1704 according to the surviving family records.
LZG4-GXY: Margaret Bernard came from Kingsthorpe in Northamptonshire, England, and immigrated to Virginia in 1655. She made her home in Old Rappahannock County, part of the early English settlement spreading along Virginia's tidewater rivers, where she lived until her death in 1687.
M198-62G: John Pemaquid Pride II was born in London and brought as an infant to Salem, Massachusetts, in 1637, growing up among the founding generation of the Bay Colony. He married in 1672 and made his home in Beverly, Essex County, where he lived to the remarkable age of more than ninety.
MH4D-LDK: Bridget Thorsley crossed from England to the new colony of Maryland in the late 1630s, among the earliest settlers of that fledgling province. Her life was brief, ending in Maryland in 1644, but she stood among the pioneer women who helped plant English settlement on the Chesapeake.
MYTB-RXD: Born in Denmark in 1742, John Johann Blume eventually made his way to the American South. When the Revolution came, he took up arms in its cause, with records of military service beginning in early 1777. He settled in Blackville Township in Barnwell County, South Carolina, where he lived out his life until his death in 1816.
